judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports fully and correctly answer all three questions with the same root causes and fixes; they are comparable, with Report 1 slightly more detailed on the docs/process gap.

report_1.evidence: `cedar_owner` is the Postgres role that owns the `cedar` database and its tables ... In Postgres, the owner of a table (and any superuser) bypasses row-level security by default, regardless of `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` being set or not — and this migration doesn't even set `FORCE`. So although `deps.py` faithfully does `SET app.current_tenant` ... the policy is never evaluated for this connection at all — the owning role sees every row unconditionally, silently, with no error.

report_2.evidence: in PostgreSQL, **row-level security policies are never applied to the table's owner** unless the table also has `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` set (it does not here — only `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, which exempts the owner by default). Since `cedar_owner` both owns the `clients`/`appointments` tables ... and is the role the FastAPI app actually connects as (per `.env`), every query the app runs ... silently bypasses the RLS policy entirely.

report_1.evidence: Change `DATABASE_URL` used by the running application ... to point at `cedar_app`, not `cedar_owner` ... `cedar_owner` should be reserved exclusively for schema/migration operations ... two separate connection strings/config values going forward, not one shared `DATABASE_URL`: - A migration-only URL using the owning role ... - An app-only URL using `cedar_app` (or equivalent least-privilege, non-bypass role)

report_2.evidence: Point the application's `DATABASE_URL` at `cedar_app`, not `cedar_owner` ... migrations (schema/DDL, run as the owning/admin role) and application runtime access (DML, run as a restricted non-owner role subject to RLS) should be strictly separated — as the repo already intended via `cedar_owner` vs `cedar_app`

report_1.evidence: The `cedar_app` role is created exclusively by `cedarline/db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which its own header comment says is "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup" — it is not invoked from `docker-compose.yml`, not from `scripts/migrate.sh`, and not from `scripts/reset-local-db.sh`. So after the volume is wiped, `reset-local-db.sh` recreates the schema ... but never recreates the `cedar_app` role, and any connection using `cedar_app` ... fails immediately with a "role does not exist" error.

report_2.evidence: `docker compose down -v` — the `-v` flag deletes the named Docker volume `cedar_pgdata` ... Nowhere in `reset-local-db.sh` is `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` re-run. That script is explicitly documented as a manual, one-time step ... So after a wipe+rebuild, the `cedar_app` role ... genuinely does not exist in the fresh cluster, and any connection attempt fails with a Postgres "role does not exist" error.
